During the ceremony, [[Academy Awards]] (commonly called the Oscars) were presented in 24 competitive categories. [[Florence Welch]] || Performers || "[[If I Rise]]" from ''[[127 Hours]]'' |- |{{sortname|Gwyneth|Paltrow}} || Performer || "[[Country Strong (soundtrack)|Coming Home]]" from ''[[Country Strong]]'' |- |{{sortname|Celine|Dion}} || Performer || "[[Smile (Charlie Chaplin song)|Smile]]" during the annual ''In Memoriam'' tribute |- |{{sortname||PS22 Chorus}} || Performers || "[[Over the Rainbow]]" from ''[[The Wizard of Oz]]'' during the closing segment |} ==Ceremony information== {{multiple image | align = right | image1 = James Franco (Cropped).jpg | width1 = 172 | alt1 = Photo of James Franco at the Austin Film Festival in 2011. | caption1 = | image2 = Anne Hathaway at MIFF.jpg | width2 = 155 | alt2 = Photo of Anne Hathaway at the Miami International Film Festival in 2014. | caption2 = | footer = [[James Franco]] (''left'') and [[Anne Hathaway]] (''right'') co-hosted the 83rd Academy Awards }} In June 2010, the AMPAS hired Oscar-winning producer [[Bruce Cohen]] and veteran television producer [[Don Mischer]] to oversee production of the telecast.{{cite news|last1=Kilday |first1=Greg |title=Bruce Cohen, Don Mischer to produce Oscars |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/bruce-cohen-don-mischer-produce-24887 |access-date=August 5, 2013 |work=The Hollywood Reporter |date=June 22, 2010 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808051226/http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/bruce-cohen-don-mischer-produce-24887 |archive-date=August 8, 2014 }} "I'm absolutely ecstatic that Bruce and Don have accepted my invitation to produce and direct the 83rd Academy Awards telecast," remarked Academy president [[Tom Sherak]]. "Their work in producing the Academy's inaugural Governors Awards was exceptional and I am confident they will bring their creative vision and extraordinary talent to produce/direct a most memorable Oscar show."{{cite news|last1=Finke |first1=Nikki |title=83rd Oscar Telecast Producers Announced |url=https://deadline.com/2010/06/huh-abc-announces-83rd-oscar-telecast-producers-48672/ |access-date=August 5, 2014 |website=Deadline Hollywood |date=June 22, 2010 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140829222839/http://deadline.com/2010/06/huh-abc-announces-83rd-oscar-telecast-producers-48672/ |archive-date=August 29, 2014 }} Although the [[82nd Academy Awards|prior ceremony]] hosted by [[Alec Baldwin]] and [[Steve Martin]] had the highest ratings in five years, their combined age was 116 years and the producers wanted to focus on a younger demographic.{{Cite web|last=Reinstein|first=Mara|date=2021-04-14|title=The Behind-the-Scenes Story of the James Franco–Anne Hathaway Oscars Debacle|url=https://www.theringer.com/movies/2021/4/14/22381783/2011-oscars-james-franco-anne-hathaway-behind-the-scenes-story|access-date=2021-04-17|website=The Ringer|language=en|archive-date=April 17, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210417220650/https://www.theringer.com/movies/2021/4/14/22381783/2011-oscars-james-franco-anne-hathaway-behind-the-scenes-story|url-status=live}} The unofficial first choice was [[Justin Timberlake]] but he declined, feeling it was at least a year too early for him. Opting for younger faces for the ceremony, Cohen and Mischer hired actor [[James Franco]] and actress [[Anne Hathaway]] as co-hosts of the 2011 ceremony.{{cite news|title=Franco and Hathaway hope to add vitality and viewers to Oscar telecast|url=https://www.stltoday.com/entertainment/movies/joe-williams/franco-and-hathaway-hope-to-add-vitality-and-viewers-to/article_f70b3212-fc58-11df-b628-0017a4a78c22.html|access-date=August 5, 2014|work=St. Louis Post Dispatch|date=November 30, 2010|archive-date=June 22, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170622044129/http://www.stltoday.com/entertainment/movies/joe-williams/franco-and-hathaway-hope-to-add-vitality-and-viewers-to/article_f70b3212-fc58-11df-b628-0017a4a78c22.html|url-status=live}} "James Franco and Anne Hathaway personify the next generation of Hollywood icons — fresh, exciting and multi-talented. We hope to create an Oscar broadcast that will both showcase their incredible talents and entertain the world on February 27," said Cohen and Mischer regarding their selections to host the gala. "We are completely thrilled that James and Anne will be joining forces with our brilliant creative team to do just that."{{cite magazine|last1=Levy |first1=Steve |title=The Most Unexpected Oscar Hosts Yet: James Franco and Anne Hathaway |url=https://newsfeed.time.com/2010/11/29/the-most-unexpected-oscar-hosts-yet-james-franco-and-anne-hathaway/ |access-date=August 5, 2014 |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |date=November 29, 2010 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140813005358/http://newsfeed.time.com/2010/11/29/the-most-unexpected-oscar-hosts-yet-james-franco-and-anne-hathaway/ |archive-date=August 13, 2014 }} Franco and Hathaway became the first male-female duo to co-host the awards show since comedian [[Jerry Lewis]] and actress [[Celeste Holm]] presided over the [[29th Academy Awards|29th ceremony]] in 1957.{{cite news|first=Brandy |last=McDonnell |title=Hugh Jackman gives Anne Hathaway, James Franco Oscar hosting advice |url=http://newsok.com/hugh-jackman-gives-anne-hathaway-james-franco-oscar-hosting-advice/article/3827298 |access-date=August 5, 2014 |work=The Oklahoman |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150402100410/http://newsok.com/hugh-jackman-gives-anne-hathaway-james-franco-oscar-hosting-advice/article/3827298 |archive-date=April 2, 2015 }} At age 28, Hathaway was also the youngest person to host an Oscar ceremony.{{cite news|last1=Pierce |first1=Scott |title=This weekend on TV: The Oscar hosts are getting younger |url=http://www.sltrib.com/sltrib/blogstv/51316798-63/cbs-hathaway-hosts-awards.html.csp |access-date=August 7, 2014 |work=[[The Salt Lake Tribune]] |date=January 27, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140810063224/http://www.sltrib.com/sltrib/blogstv/51316798-63/cbs-hathaway-hosts-awards.html.csp |archive-date=August 10, 2014 }} Furthermore, AMPAS announced that this year's ceremony was "the most interactive awards show in history". The Academy revamped their official website oscar.com to include lists of all the nominees and winners, as well as film trailers and exclusive video content produced by both AMPAS and Oscar telecaster ABC.{{cite news|last1=Pond |first1=Steve |title=A Guide to the Academy's Interactive Oscar Features |url=https://www.thewrap.com/awards/column-post/guide-academys-interactive-oscar-features-25050/ |access-date=August 7, 2014 |website=[[TheWrap]] |date=February 25, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140810125801/http://www.thewrap.com/awards/column-post/guide-academys-interactive-oscar-features-25050/ |archive-date=August 10, 2014 }} Also, via the Academy's Twitter and Facebook pages, people could post questions for any actor or celebrity attending the festivities to answer. One of the four Oscar pre-show co-hosts would then pose selected questions to both nominees and attendees alike. For a fee of US$4.99, users had online access to two dozen video streams that would take them from the red carpet, through the ceremony and on to the post-telecast Governors Ball. Several of the cameras utilized 360-degree views that viewers could direct.{{cite web|author=Sheehan, Paul |title=Oscars offer unprecedented backstage access |url=http://www.goldderby.com/films/news/1166/oscars-offer-unprecedented-backstage-access.html |publisher=Gold Derby. |date=February 18, 2011 |access-date=April 19, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110222191518/http://www.goldderby.com/films/news/1166/oscars-offer-unprecedented-backstage-access.html |archive-date=February 22, 2011 }} Several other people participated in the production of the ceremony. For a younger writer, Franco consulted [[Judd Apatow]], who suggested [[Jordan Rubin]] who brought in [[Megan Amram]]. [[William Ross (composer)|William Ross]] served as musical director and conductor for the ceremony.{{cite news|title=William Ross to Conduct Oscars This Year |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/william-ross-conduct-oscars-year-160718 |access-date=September 18, 2011 |work=The Hollywood Reporter |date=February 23, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110226042247/http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/william-ross-conduct-oscars-year-160718 |archive-date=February 26, 2011 }} Production designer Steve Bass built a new stage design for the ceremony.{{cite news|last1=Kilday |first1=Greg |title=Steve Bass hired for Oscar show |access-date=August 7, 2014 |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/steve-bass-hired-oscar-show-28254 |work=The Hollywood Reporter |date=September 23, 2010 |url-status=live and TV personality Dave Karger greeted guests entering the red carpet.{{cite news|title=Dave Karger Takes Army Archerd's Old Red-Carpet Oscar Post |url=https://www.thewrap.com/dave-karger-takes-army-archerds-old-red-carpet-oscar-post-24971/ |access-date=August 5, 2014 |first=Steve |last=Pond |website=TheWrap |date=February 23, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161223093712/http://www.thewrap.com/dave-karger-takes-army-archerds-old-red-carpet-oscar-post-24971/ |archive-date=December 23, 2016 }} Designer Marc Friedland designed a new envelope heralding the winner of each category made from a high-gloss iridescent metallic gold paper stock, with red-lacquered lining that featured the Oscar |url=https://newsfeed.time.com/2011/02/17/just-what-the-oscars-need-a-new-envelope/ |magazine=Time |date=February 17, 2011 |access-date=April 19, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110421084605/http://newsfeed.time.com/2011/02/17/just-what-the-oscars-need-a-new-envelope/ |archive-date=April 21, 2011 }}{{cite news|url=http://latimesblogs.latimes.com/awards/2011/02/oscars-the-envelope-gets-a-new-design-.html |title=Oscars: The envelope gets a new design |author=King, Susan |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 16, 2011 |access-date=April 19, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110319045708/http://latimesblogs.latimes.com/awards/2011/02/oscars-the-envelope-gets-a-new-design-.html |archive-date=March 19, 2011 }} During the run-up to the ceremony, television personality [[Chris Harrison]] hosted "Road to the Oscars", a weekly behind-the-scenes video blog.{{cite magazine|last1=Karger |first1=Dave |title=Academy launches 'The Road to the Oscars' |url=https://www.ew.com/article/2011/02/18/academy-launches-the-road-to-the-oscars |access-date=July 14, 2011 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly|date=February 18, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150402145547/http://www.ew.com/article/2011/02/18/academy-launches-the-road-to-the-oscars |archive-date=April 2, 2015 }} [[Ben Mankiewicz]] hosted the official ABC pre-show, giving professional betting odds for the winners. [[PS22 Chorus]] children's choir performed "[[Over the Rainbow]]" from ''[[The Wizard of Oz]]'' at the end of the ceremony.{{cite news|last1=Maerz |first1=Melissa |title=New York's P.S. 22 choir to go 'Over the Rainbow' at the Oscars |url=https://www.latimes.com/entertainment/la-xpm-2011-feb-14-la-et-oscar-choir-20110214-story.html |access-date=March 25, 2015 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 14, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150402125528/http://articles.latimes.com/2011/feb/14/entertainment/la-et-oscar-choir-20110214 |archive-date=April 2, 2015 }} According to Rubin, Hathaway was heavily involved during the month of preparation. Franco on the other hand was busy shooting movies, while teaching a class and getting both his masters and his PhD. When filming started, Hathaway was focused and determined while Franco was more laid back, causing friction. In the closing weeks, Franco went back to Apatow who hired four additional writers; in response, Hathaway brought in [[Liz Feldman]]. This resulted in last-minute scrambling. According to Amram, "[a] lot of stuff that made it into the show was written a few days beforehand." Franco immediately left after the show ended, catching a flight to make a morning seminar on medieval manuscripts at [[Yale]].{{Cite news|last=Foderaro|first=Lisa W.|date=2011-03-04|title=James Franco Straddles Two Roles at Yale|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2011/03/04/nyregion/04franco.html|access-date=2021-04-17|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=April 17, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210417224544/https://www.nytimes.com/2011/03/04/nyregion/04franco.html|url-status=live}} ===Box office performance of nominated films=== For the second consecutive year, the field of major nominees included at least one [[Blockbuster (entertainment)|blockbuster]] at the American and Canadian box offices. However, only three of the nominees had grossed over $100 million before the nominations were announced, compared with five from the previous year.{{cite news|last1=Subers |first1=Ray |title=2010 Academy Award Nominations |url=https://www.boxofficemojo.com/news/?id=3057&p=.htm |access-date=August 7, 2014 |website=[[Box Office Mojo]] ([[Amazon.com]]) |date=January 25, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140714204309/http://boxofficemojo.com/news/?id=3057&p=.htm |archive-date=July 14, 2014 }} The combined gross of the ten Best Picture nominees when the Oscars were announced was $1.2 billion, the second-highest ever behind 2009. The average gross was $119.3 million. Two of the ten Best Picture nominees were among the top ten releases in box office during the nominations. At the time of the announcement of nominations on January 25, ''Toy Story 3'' was the highest-grossing film among the Best Picture nominees with $414.9 million in domestic box office receipts.{{cite news|url=http://newsok.com/box-office-numbers-for-oscar-best-picture-nominees/article/feed/237509 |work=The Oklahoman |title=Box-office numbers for Oscar best-picture nominees |access-date=January 27, 2011 |date=January 25, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150402114604/http://newsok.com/box-office-numbers-for-oscar-best-picture-nominees/article/feed/237509 |archive-date=April 2, 2015 }} The only other top ten box office hit to receive a nomination was ''Inception'' which earned $292.5 million. Among the remaining eight nominees, ''True Grit'' was the next-highest-grossing film with $137.9 million followed by ''The Social Network'' ($95.4 million), ''Black Swan'' ($83.2 million), ''The Fighter'' ($72.6 million), ''The King's Speech'' ($57.3 million), ''The Kids Are All Right'' ($20.8 million), ''127 Hours'' ($11.2 million), and finally ''Winter's Bone'' ($6.2 million). Of the top 50 grossing movies of the year, 55 nominations went to 15 films on the list. Only ''Toy Story 3'' (1st), ''Inception'' (5th), ''How to Train Your Dragon'' (9th), ''True Grit'' (17th), ''The Social Network'' (29th), ''The Town'' (32nd), ''Black Swan'' (38th), and ''The Fighter'' (45th) were nominated for directing, acting, screenwriting, Best Picture or Animated Feature.{{cite web|url=https://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2011-01-24&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2010&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |title=2010 Box Office Grosses (as of January 24, 2011) |website=Box Office Mojo (Amazon.com) |access-date=April 22, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150415163247/http://www.boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2011-01-24&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2010&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |archive-date=April 15, 2015 }} The other top-50 box office hits that earned nominations were ''Alice in Wonderland'' (2nd), ''Iron Man 2'' (3rd), ''Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows — Part 1'' (6th), ''Tangled'' (10th), ''Tron: Legacy'' (12th), ''Salt'' (21st), and ''Unstoppable'' (39th). ===Critical reviews=== The show received a negative reception from most media publications. According to writer [[Bruce Vilanch]], the crowd enjoyed the starting short film, but when Franco and Hathaway came on stage, it shifted. According to Rubin, Hathaway "was embracing their arrival on stage" while Franco was filming the crowd on his phone. Mara Reinstein of ''[[The Ringer (website)|The Ringer]]'' said there was no single moment of failure but described the broadcast as "death by a thousand paper cuts." Film critic [[Roger Ebert]] said, "Despite the many worthy nominated films, the Oscarcast was painfully dull, slow, witless, and hosted by the ill-matched James Franco and Anne Hathaway. She might have made a delightful foil for another partner, but Franco had a deer-in-the-headlights manner and read his lines robotically." He went on to praise the winners of the night, but he ended his review with the words, "Dead. In. The. Water."{{cite news|url=http://rogerebert.suntimes.com/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=/20110227/OSCARS/110229986 |title=Oscars: "King" wins, show loses |author-link=Roger Ebert |last=Ebert |first=Roger |access-date=February 28, 2011 |date=February 27, 2011 |work=Chicago Sun-Times |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110302092730/http://rogerebert.suntimes.com/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=%2F20110227%2FOSCARS%2F110229986 |archive-date=March 2, 2011 }} Writer [[David Wild]] called it "the world's most uncomfortable blind date between the cool rocker stoner kid and the adorable theater camp cheerleader." Television critic Tim Goodman of ''[[The Hollywood Reporter]]'' commented, "In what could go down as one of the worst Oscar telecasts in history, a bad and risky idea — letting two actors host — played out in spectacularly unwatchable fashion on the biggest of all nights for the film world." He also added, "These Oscars were a bore-fest that seemed to drag on relentlessly but listlessly."{{cite news|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/review/franco-bombs-at-oscars-makes-162234 |title=83rd Annual Academy Awards: Television Review |first1=Tim |last1=Goodman |work=The Hollywood Reporter |date=February 28, 2011 |access-date=February 28, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110301161603/http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/review/franco-bombs-at-oscars-makes-162234 |archive-date=March 1, 2011 }} Gail Pennington of the ''[[St. Louis Post-Dispatch]]'' wrote that the ceremony "felt a little like a bad night on ''[[Saturday Night Live]]'' — awkward, slow and not particularly entertaining." Regarding the hosts, she quipped that Hathaway "at least tried", but she remarked, "Franco seemed half asleep, or possibly stoned."{{cite news|last1=Pennington|first1=Gail|title=Oscar show goes on (and on)|url=https://www.stltoday.com/entertainment/television/gail-pennington/oscar-show-goes-on-and-on/article_2825d3d6-42e3-11e0-8a17-0017a4a78c22.html|access-date=September 18, 2011|work=St. Louis Post-Dispatch|date=February 28, 2011|archive-date=June 22, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170622044129/http://www.stltoday.com/entertainment/television/gail-pennington/oscar-show-goes-on-and-on/article_2825d3d6-42e3-11e0-8a17-0017a4a78c22.html|url-status=live}} Some media outlets received the broadcast more positively. ''[[Entertainment Weekly]]'' television critic [[Ken Tucker]] stated that the show was "Funny, poised, relaxed, and smart, Anne Hathaway and James Franco made for marvelous Oscar hosts. Their combination of respect and informality struck the right tone for the night, a happily surprising production that had its share of fine moments both planned and ad-libbed." On the overall aspect of the ceremony, they concluded "all in all, it was a fun, briskly paced night."{{cite magazine|url=http://watching-tv.ew.com/2011/02/28/oscars-kings-speech-franco-hathaway/ |title=The Oscar telecast review: The hosts, the speeches, the mistakes, the ad-libs... |magazine=Entertainment Weekly|first=Ken |last=Tucker |date=February 28, 2011 |access-date=February 28, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110301042728/http://watching-tv.ew.com/2011/02/28/oscars-kings-speech-franco-hathaway/ |archive-date=March 1, 2011 }} Mary McNamara from the ''[[Los Angeles Times]]'' commented, "The two seemed to be following the directive to "first do no harm," as if they knew they couldn't score as big as [[Jimmy Fallon]] did with the [[62nd Primetime Emmy Awards|Emmy Awards]], but were determined to avoid becoming morning show fodder like [[Ricky Gervais]] was after [[68th Golden Globe Awards|this year's Golden Globes]]. The result was a show that moved along, with a few draggy bits and high notes, like precisely what it was: a very long and fancy awards show." Her review further said "Overall, the evening had an oddly business-like feel, a mind-numbing evenness that was exacerbated by the relentless predictability of the winners, and the fact that none of the acting winners were played off no matter how long their "thank-yous" went."{{cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/entertainment/tv/la-et-oscar-tv-20110228-story.html |title=Television Review: Anne Hathaway and James Franco play it safe |first=Mary |last=McNamara |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 28, 2011 |access-date=February 28, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110308153750/http://articles.latimes.com/2011/feb/28/entertainment/la-et-oscar-tv-20110228 |archive-date=March 8, 2011 }} ===Ratings and reception=== The In addition, the program scored an 11.8 rating over a 30 share among the 18–49 demographic, which was a 12 percent decrease over last year's demographic numbers.{{cite news|last1=Kissell |first1=Rick |title=Oscars ratings down from last year |url=https://variety.com/2011/tv/news/oscar-ratings-down-from-last-year-1118033008/ |access-date=August 4, 2014 |work=Variety |date=February 28, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140812202530/http://variety.com/2011/tv/news/oscar-ratings-down-from-last-year-1118033008/ |archive-date=August 12, 2014 }} == ''In Memoriam'' == The ''In Memoriam'' tribute, which featured [[Celine Dion]] performing the [[Charlie Chaplin]] song "[[Smile (Charlie Chaplin song)|Smile]]", paid tribute to the following individuals.{{cite news |last1=Burson |first1=Jeff |title=Reviewing the Black Carpet at 2011 Oscars |url=https://www.al.com/finaltaxi/2011/03/reviewing_the_black_carpet_at.html |access-date=March 25, 2021 |work=[[The Birmingham News]] |date=March 1, 2011 |archive-date=April 18, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210418082647/https://www.al.com/finaltaxi/2011/03/reviewing_the_black_carpet_at.html |url-status=live }} {{div col|colwidth=22em}} * [[John Barry (composer)|John Barry]] – Composer * [[Grant McCune]] – Visual effects * [[Tony Curtis]] – Actor * [[Edward Limato]] – Agent * [[Tom Mankiewicz]] – Writer * [[Gloria Stuart]] – Actress * [[William Fraker]] – Cinematographer * [[Joseph Strick]] – Director * [[Lionel Jeffries]] – Actor * [[Sally Menke]] – Editor * [[Ronni Chasen]] – Publicist * [[Leslie Nielsen]] – Actor * [[Robert Radnitz]] – Producer * [[Claude Chabrol]] – Director * [[Pete Postlethwaite]] – Actor * [[Bill Littlejohn]] – Animator * [[Pierre Guffroy]] – Art director * [[Patricia Neal]] – Actress * [[George Hickenlooper]] – Director * [[Irving Ravetch]] – Writer * [[Robert Culp]] – Actor * [[Robert F.
